seasonal_wine.name: Apricot Blossom Season and Early Steinfeder Release
seasonal_wine.description: April brings the Wachauer Marille apricot blossom, turning the valley pink-white and signalling the official start of the wine-tourism season. Cellars open to visitors, and the lightest Steinfeder wines from the prior autumn's harvest are poured in tasting rooms and Heuriger. The combination of blossoming orchards and early-vintage whites draws tourists from Vienna along the Danube by boat and bicycle.
seasonal_wine.what_to_drink: Steinfeder Gruner Veltliner and Steinfeder Riesling from the most recent harvest, with their delicate aromatic freshness intact.
seasonal_wine.visitor_tip: Book river cruise tickets from Vienna early; boats fill quickly during blossom peak weekends.

seasonal_wine.name: Federspiel Season: May to June Release Window
seasonal_wine.description: The Federspiel tier, representing medium-bodied dry whites in the Wachau's Codex classification, is typically released between May and June of the year following harvest. The release coincides with the growing season when the vines are just leafing out, and producers invite visitors for cellar tastings of the freshly bottled Federspiel wines. The mineral-driven Riesling Federspiel and the herbal Gruner Veltliner Federspiel are the workhorse wines of the region at this time.
seasonal_wine.what_to_drink: Federspiel Riesling and Federspiel Gruner Veltliner from the most recent vintage, ideal for the asparagus season that peaks in the Danube valley in May.
seasonal_wine.visitor_tip: Federspiel is available at producers' direct-sales counters; the cooperative Domane Wachau offers generous tasting access in Durnstein.

seasonal_wine.name: White Asparagus Pairing Season, May
seasonal_wine.description: The Danube valley's white asparagus (Spargel) harvest in May is closely tied to the Wachau wine calendar. Local restaurants and Heuriger design seasonal menus around asparagus paired with Federspiel and Steinfeder Gruner Veltliner, whose fresh herbal character and moderate acidity are a classic match for the delicate vegetable. The combination drives a dedicated food-and-wine tourism segment each May.
seasonal_wine.what_to_drink: Steinfeder or Federspiel Gruner Veltliner, particularly from producers in Weissenkirchen, Spitz, and Mautern who offer the pairing on seasonal menus.
seasonal_wine.visitor_tip: Ask for the Spargelkarte (asparagus menu) at Heuriger from late April; the season typically runs four to five weeks.

seasonal_wine.name: Smaragd Back-Vintage Tasting Season, July to August
seasonal_wine.description: Summer is the peak tourist season in the Wachau, with cycling and Danube cruise visitors swelling the valley. At this time of year, producers and wine bars pour aged Smaragd wines from previous vintages that have had time to develop. The heat of summer suits the richer, more substantial Smaragd Riesling and Gruner Veltliner, and vertical tasting flights comparing three or four back vintages are a signature offering.
seasonal_wine.what_to_drink: Aged Smaragd Riesling from three to eight years past harvest, such as Kellerberg or Loibenberg single-vineyard bottlings showing honeyed mineral complexity.
seasonal_wine.visitor_tip: Several Spitz and Weissenkirchen producers offer vertical tastings by appointment in July and August; book ahead, capacity is limited.

seasonal_wine.name: Wachau Gourmet Festival Spring Edition
seasonal_wine.description: The Wachau Gourmet Festival typically takes place in April or early May each year, staged across historic venues in Loiben and Durnstein including the cellars at Loibnerhof and Schloss Durnstein. Producers pour current-release and library Smaragd wines alongside dishes prepared by invited chefs, making it the primary showcase for the vintage's Federspiel and newly released Smaragd. The multi-night format includes gala dinners and cellar tours.
seasonal_wine.what_to_drink: Current-release Smaragd Gruner Veltliner and Riesling alongside the previous year's Federspiel, with producer-hosted cellar pours throughout the multi-day event.
seasonal_wine.visitor_tip: Gala dinners sell out months in advance; day-pass cellar walk tickets offer broader access at lower cost.

seasonal_wine.name: Harvest Opens: Steinfeder Picking, Late September
seasonal_wine.description: Harvest in the Wachau typically begins in late September with the picking of grapes destined for Steinfeder, the lightest tier of the Codex. At this stage of harvest, grapes have reached natural ripeness without excessive concentration, preserving the bright acidity and floral character that defines the category. Terraced plots are harvested by hand on account of the near-vertical gradient, making harvest labour-intensive and highly weather-dependent.
seasonal_wine.what_to_drink: Freshly pressed grape juice (Sturm) available at Heuriger during harvest week as a seasonal delicacy, ahead of the Steinfeder wines released the following spring.
seasonal_wine.visitor_tip: Vineyards above Weissenkirchen and Joching are particularly scenic during early harvest; visiting mid-week avoids weekend crowds.

seasonal_wine.name: Federspiel Harvest: Early October
seasonal_wine.description: The Federspiel tier is harvested in early October after the Steinfeder lots. At this stage, grapes destined for the middle tier have accumulated the body and extract needed for the characteristic falconer-light style that characterises Federspiel: more substance than Steinfeder but without the richness of Smaragd. The hand harvest on the stone terraces continues through the steep parcels above Loiben, Joching, and Spitz.
seasonal_wine.what_to_drink: The previous year's Federspiel Riesling and Gruner Veltliner during harvest season while the cellar receives new fruit, with the contrast of current and prior vintages instructive.
seasonal_wine.visitor_tip: Wachau harvest weekends in early October often include open-cellar events; check the Vinea Wachau events calendar for participating producers.

seasonal_wine.name: Smaragd Harvest: Mid to Late October
seasonal_wine.description: Smaragd grapes are harvested last, often into late October when phenolic ripeness is at its peak. The lizard-named tier requires grapes to reach a minimum must weight, and warm autumns in recent years have pushed harvest into November for some Riesling parcels on the highest and most north-facing terraces. The patience and selective picking required make Smaragd the most labour-intensive and weather-sensitive tier.
seasonal_wine.what_to_drink: Two- to three-year-old Smaragd wines showing their initial development while the new Smaragd from the just-completed harvest rests in the cellar.
seasonal_wine.visitor_tip: Late-October visits offer the chance to taste Sturm (fermenting must) directly at producers who accept walk-ins; call ahead as cellar access is restricted during active fermentation.

seasonal_wine.name: Weinherbst Niederosterreich (Lower Austria Wine Autumn)
seasonal_wine.description: The Weinherbst Niederosterreich (Lower Austria's Wine Autumn) runs from September through October, encompassing the harvest celebrations across the entire Lower Austria wine zone including the Wachau. Participating villages and producers host open-cellar days, harvest markets, and walking tours through the vineyards. The Wachau portion of the celebration is particularly scenic during the leaf-colour change, when the terraced vines turn gold and orange against the Danube cliffs.
seasonal_wine.what_to_drink: The full range of Lower Austrian varieties during the autumn celebrations: aged Wachau Smaragd alongside Kremstal Riesling and Kamptal Gruner Veltliner from the day-trip appellations.
seasonal_wine.visitor_tip: The Weinherbst app and website list all participating producers across Lower Austria; downloading it before visiting the Wachau allows spontaneous route planning.

seasonal_wine.name: Vinea Wachau Open-Cellar Days, October
seasonal_wine.description: Each October the Vinea Wachau association organises open-cellar days across its member estates, allowing visitors to taste directly from barrel and tank while the harvest ferments. The event spans multiple weekends and covers producers from Mautern and Durnstein in the east to Spitz and Weissenkirchen in the west. Entry to individual cellar tastings is typically included with a glass purchase.
seasonal_wine.what_to_drink: Young fermenting Gruner Veltliner and Riesling tasted as Sturm (unfinished) and alongside the prior release's Federspiel and Smaragd at the same producer.
seasonal_wine.visitor_tip: The Durnstein cluster of producers (Domane Wachau, Schmelz, and Chorherren) makes for an easy car-free afternoon if combined with the bus from Krems.

seasonal_wine.name: Krems Christmas Markets with Wachau Wine, December
seasonal_wine.description: The city of Krems at the eastern gateway of the Wachau hosts advent and Christmas markets each December, where stalls pour local Wachau Federspiel and Smaragd wines alongside mulled wine (Gluhwein) and seasonal foods. The historic inner city of Krems-Stein provides the backdrop for several markets, and local wine retailers sell gift bottles of Wachau producers' current releases.
seasonal_wine.what_to_drink: Aged Smaragd Riesling from two to four years back with local Pfarrkirchen smoked trout, or a warming glass of Kremser Stadtgraben Riesling from the city's own vineyard.
seasonal_wine.visitor_tip: The Stein wine quarter (Steiner Landstrasse) runs its own small market alongside the main Krems market; the two are a ten-minute walk apart.

seasonal_wine.name: New Smaragd Vintage Release Window, Spring Following Harvest
seasonal_wine.description: Wachau Smaragd wines are typically held in the cellar through winter and released to trade and visitors between April and June of the year following harvest. This release window allows the wines at least six months of tank or barrel ageing after the October harvest and means that the current-vintage Smaragd available for tasting in spring represents the newest commercial expression of the top tier. Producers often release single-vineyard Smaragd bottlings slightly later, in May or June.
seasonal_wine.what_to_drink: New-release Smaragd Gruner Veltliner from producers like F.X. Pichler, Knoll, or Prager, tasted in the first weeks of release to capture their primary fruit character before development.
seasonal_wine.visitor_tip: Several producers hold formal new-vintage release events in April or May; sign up to producer mailing lists for invitations.
